nodes.name: welcome
nodes.content_template:
Welcome to yap-orange, a hierarchical note-taking system with graph linking.

Everything in yap-orange is a **block** — organized in a tree, connected by links, and extended with custom types. Explore these sections to learn how it works:

- {0} — blocks, namespaces, and tree structure
- {1} — wiki-links, resolution, and backlinks
- {2} — custom schemas and typed blocks
- {3} — edges and semantic relationships
- {4} — drag-drop, keyboard shortcuts, and navigation

nodes.name: hierarchy
nodes.content_template: Blocks and namespaces form the structural backbone of yap-orange. Everything is a block, organized in a tree.

nodes.name: linking
nodes.content_template: Wiki-links connect blocks across the hierarchy. Links target stable lineage IDs, so they survive moves and renames.

nodes.name: types
nodes.content_template: Custom types extend blocks with structured data through schemas, type registries, and embedded type instances.

nodes.name: graph
nodes.content_template: Edges create semantic relationships between blocks that exist outside the hierarchical tree. Visualize them in the Graph panel.

nodes.name: organizing
nodes.content_template: Keyboard shortcuts, drag-and-drop, and context menus let you restructure content efficiently.

nodes.name: blocks
nodes.content_template:
A **block** is the fundamental unit of content. Each block has:

- A **name** displayed in the sidebar and outliner
- **Content** — Markdown text with wiki-links
- **Properties** — key-value metadata (open the Properties panel with Alt+4)
- A **content type** — text, schema, setting, or custom

Blocks are stored as immutable snapshots called **atoms**. When you edit a block, a new atom is created and the block's lineage pointer updates to the latest version. Every edit is preserved in history.

nodes.name: namespaces
nodes.content_template:
Namespaces are blocks that contain other blocks. The path `tutorial::hierarchy::namespaces` means this block is a child of {0}, which is a child of the tutorial root.

Namespace paths use `::` as the separator. Any block can be referenced by its full path in a wiki-link.

nodes.name: creating blocks
nodes.content_template:
To create a new block:

1. Navigate to a namespace in the sidebar
2. Click the **+** button or press **Enter** in the outliner
3. Type a name and press **Enter** to start editing
4. Press **Escape** or **Cmd+Enter** to save

New blocks appear as children of the currently selected namespace. You can also use {0} to move blocks between namespaces after creation.

nodes.name: editing
nodes.content_template:
The editor uses CodeMirror 6 with Markdown support. Key features:

- **Wiki-links** — type `[[` to start a link (see {0})
- **Embeds** — type `![[` to embed another block inline
- **Typed entries** — type `@type{...}` to create a typed entry (see {1})
- **Escape** saves and exits edit mode
- **Shift+Enter** inserts a newline within the block
- **Enter** creates a new sibling block below

nodes.name: nesting
nodes.content_template:
Blocks can be nested to any depth. Use {0} to indent blocks under their siblings, creating a deeper hierarchy.

The **sidebar** shows the top-level namespace tree. The **outliner** shows the full depth of the selected namespace. You can expand and collapse branches in both views.

nodes.name: wiki-link syntax
nodes.content_template:
Wiki-links connect blocks using `[[path::to::block]]` syntax. When you type `[[`, the editor suggests matching blocks.

Path formats:
- `[[tutorial::hierarchy::blocks]]` — absolute path to {0}
- `[[./child]]` — relative link to a child of the current block
- `[[../sibling]]` — relative link to a sibling
- `[["name with::colons"]]` — quoted segments for names containing `::`

nodes.name: link resolution
nodes.content_template:
Links target **lineage IDs**, not paths. When you write `[[tutorial::hierarchy::blocks]]`, the system resolves it to the block's stable lineage ID at save time.

This means:
- **Moving** a block doesn't break links to it
- **Renaming** a block doesn't break links
- Links survive edits because atoms are immutable and lineages persist

Unresolved links (to blocks that don't exist yet) are preserved as text and will auto-resolve once the target is created.

nodes.name: backlinks
nodes.content_template:
The **Links panel** (Alt+3) shows all blocks that reference the current block. This is the reverse of wiki-links — instead of seeing where this block points, you see what points here.

Backlinks update automatically as content changes. Use them to discover unexpected connections and navigate the knowledge graph.

nodes.name: link examples
nodes.content_template:
Here are live links demonstrating the syntax. Click any to navigate:

- {0} — explains how yap-orange organizes content
- {1} — covers the link syntax you're reading about
- {2} — shows how to create custom data types
- {3} — lists keyboard navigation shortcuts

Notice how each link renders as a clickable reference. The Links panel (Alt+3) on any of these targets will show this block as a backlink.

nodes.name: what are types
nodes.content_template:
Custom types extend blocks with structured data. A type is defined by a **schema** — a list of fields with names and types.

An **entry** is a block of a given type. Create one by typing `@typeName{"field":"value"}` in a new block. Two built-in types are included:
- **todo** — task tracking with status cycling (todo → doing → done), a description field, and time tracking
- **person** — contact information with name, email, birthday, and notes

Typed blocks have two display modes: **nav mode** shows a compact inline summary (e.g. `[checkbox] STATUS description...`); **edit mode** shows the full form. Click or press Enter to edit, Escape to save and exit — the same pattern as regular blocks.

nodes.name: type registry
nodes.content_template:
The type registry lives at `types` in the root namespace. It has `content_type = "type_registry"` and displays a management UI listing all defined schemas.

To create a new type:
1. Navigate to the `types` namespace in the sidebar
2. The registry view shows existing types with field summaries
3. Use the creation form at the bottom to add a new type name
4. Click the new type to open its {0}

nodes.name: creating schemas
nodes.content_template:
A schema defines the fields for a custom type. Click a schema to see its compact field summary (nav mode), then click or press Enter to open the field editor (edit mode).

The field editor lets you:
- Add fields with a **name** and **type** (string, number, boolean, date, enum, ref, text)
- Mark fields as **required**
- Define **enum options** (comma-separated values)
- Set a **target type** for ref fields

Once defined, create a typed entry by typing `@typeName{"field":"value"}` as the entire content of a new block. On save, the block's content type and properties are set automatically.

nodes.name: what are edges
nodes.content_template:
Edges are semantic relationships between blocks that exist outside the hierarchical tree. While the parent-child tree defines **structure**, edges define **meaning**.

Each edge has:
- A **source** block (from)
- A **target** block (to)
- A **type** (e.g., related-to, depends-on, inspired-by)
- Optional **properties** (key-value metadata)

Unlike wiki-links in content, edges are first-class objects visible in the Graph panel (Alt+5).

nodes.name: creating edges
nodes.content_template:
To create an edge:
1. Select a block in the outliner
2. Open the **Graph panel** (Alt+5)
3. The graph shows existing connections for the selected block
4. Use the edge creation controls to add new relationships

Edges appear bidirectionally in the graph view. You can also see incoming edges in the Links panel alongside {0}.

nodes.name: edge types
nodes.content_template:
Edge types are free-form strings that categorize relationships. Common types:

- **related-to** — general association
- **depends-on** — dependency relationship
- **inspired-by** — creative influence
- **extends** — builds upon another concept
- **references** — cites or mentions

You can use any string as an edge type. The Graph panel visualizes different types to help you see the structure of your knowledge base.

nodes.name: drag and drop
nodes.content_template:
Drag blocks in the outliner to reorganize content:

- **Drop on a block** — makes it a child (nesting)
- **Drop between blocks** — reorders siblings
- **Drop on the sidebar** — moves to a different namespace

You can also drag external files (images, PDFs, documents) onto the outliner to attach them as media blocks. Drag-and-drop preserves all links. Since links target lineage IDs (not paths), moving a block never breaks references to it.

nodes.name: indenting
nodes.content_template:
Use **Tab** and **Shift+Tab** in the outliner to indent and outdent blocks:

- **Tab** — indent: makes the block a child of the previous sibling
- **Shift+Tab** — outdent: moves the block up one level in the hierarchy

This is the keyboard equivalent of {0}. Combined with arrow keys, you can restructure content entirely from the keyboard.

nodes.name: keyboard shortcuts
nodes.content_template:
**Panel navigation:**
- Alt+1 — Navigator (sidebar)
- Alt+2 — Bookmarks
- Alt+3 — Links (backlinks)
- Alt+4 — Properties
- Alt+5 — Graph
- Ctrl+K — Quick Switcher (MRU panel list)

**Outliner (navigate mode):**
- Arrow Up/Down — Move selection
- Arrow Right/Left — Expand/collapse
- Enter — Edit selected block
- Tab / Shift+Tab — Indent / outdent
- Delete — Delete selected block

**Editor (edit mode):**
- Escape or Cmd+Enter — Save and exit
- Enter — New sibling block
- Shift+Enter — Newline within block
- `[[` — Wiki-link completion
- `![[` — Embed another block inline
- `@type{...}` — Create typed entry

nodes.name: context menu
nodes.content_template:
Right-click any block in the outliner or sidebar to access the context menu:

- **Edit** — enter edit mode
- **Rename** — change the block name
- **Delete** — soft-delete the block (restorable)
- **Copy link** — copy the wiki-link path to clipboard
- **Attach file...** — attach an image, PDF, or document as a child block
- **Import from file...** — import a yap export (JSON or ZIP)
- **Export** — export the subtree as JSON or ZIP

Deleted blocks can be restored. Links to deleted blocks are preserved and will work again after restoration.

nodes.name: tips and tricks
nodes.content_template:
A few tips to get the most out of yap-orange:

1. **Quick Switcher** (Ctrl+K) — jump between panels instantly
2. **Bookmarks** (Alt+2) — pin frequently used blocks for fast access
3. **Properties** (Alt+4) — add metadata like tags, status, or dates to any block
4. **Graph view** (Alt+5) — visualize connections between blocks
5. **Import/Export** — back up subtrees as JSON or ZIP, share them between instances
6. **Embeds** — use `![[path]]` to embed images or other blocks inline

This tutorial was auto-imported as seed data on first run. Feel free to edit, reorganize, or delete it.

properties.description: Try editing this todo — click or press Enter to see the full form with status, description, and time fields
